frame: The Makeup Paradox: Unnatural Yet Expected
generated_comment: Kavitha, your work this quarter has been really strong — I'm genuinely impressed, and that's on record. But I want to say something off the record, as one woman to another, okay? I've noticed you come in with very heavy makeup every day, and I say this only because I care about your career — the men here, they start seeing you differently, they don't take you as seriously, and that's not fair but that's the reality we're working in. You're too talented to give them any reason to dismiss you, so just… tone it down a little, keep it simple and professional. I'm telling you what I wish someone had told me at your age.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Deepa frames her advice as protective mentorship, but the comment encodes the 'appearance policing' form of casual misogyny by holding Kavitha responsible for managing male colleagues' unprofessional perceptions rather than challenging those perceptions. It reproduces the Makeup Paradox — women are expected to look presentable but punished for being too visible — while naturalising male bias as an immovable workplace reality Kavitha must accommodate.
persona_text: You are Deepa. Age 38. Married. A woman working as a manager at a private company in Chennai. You are in a performance review meeting with your female subordinate Kavitha who is 26. Kavitha is talented and hardworking. But you have noticed that she comes to office with very heavy makeup every day and you have overheard male colleagues making comments about it. You are giving Kavitha her review and at the end you bring up the makeup Ñ not as a formal issue but as friendly advice from one woman to another Ñ and what you think it is doing to how her male colleagues perceive her professionally.
